title: Listening - Part 4: Studying Volcanoes
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a scientist called Sofia, who studies volcanic activity. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m speaking to Dr Sofia Alvarez, a scientist who studies volcanoes. Sofia, when did you first become interested in them? || Sofia: People expect me to say I watched a dramatic eruption on television, but that wasn’t it. [24]On a school geography trip, our teacher showed us a quiet hillside made from volcanic rock and explained how the entire landscape had been formed[/24]. I was amazed that something which seemed so still had such a powerful history. || Presenter: Does your job involve standing near erupting volcanoes? || Sofia: Only occasionally, and always with careful safety rules. A great deal of my time is less adventurous than people imagine. [25]Most working days are spent examining measurements on a computer rather than climbing over hot rocks[/25]. We look for tiny changes in gas, ground movement and temperature that may tell us a volcano is becoming active. || Presenter: What is the hardest part of the work? || Sofia: The science is challenging, but explaining it can be harder. Communities understandably want definite answers. [26]Our greatest difficulty is telling people honestly about risk when we cannot state exactly if or when an eruption will happen[/26]. You need to be clear without making people panic or giving false reassurance. || Presenter: Has technology changed your research? || Sofia: Completely. We still install instruments on the ground, but we can now use drones. [27]They collect samples and images from areas that would be too dangerous for scientists to enter on foot[/27]. That gives us information earlier and also means nobody has to take unnecessary risks for a measurement. || Presenter: Have you ever been present during an eruption? || Sofia: Yes, during a fairly small one several years ago. It was visually extraordinary, but I didn’t feel excited in the way tourists might. [28]I kept thinking about the families who had left their homes and were waiting to hear whether it was safe to return[/28]. Studying volcanoes involves real people, not just beautiful photographs. || Presenter: You work with researchers from several countries. Why is that important? || Sofia: Volcanoes vary, but warning signs often have similarities. [29]By comparing information from eruptions in different places, we improve our ability to recognise changes early[/29]. International teams also share expensive equipment and experience, which matters because many active volcanoes are in regions with limited resources. || Presenter: What advice do you have for students interested in your career? || Sofia: Enjoy geography, certainly, but don’t imagine it is only fieldwork. [30]Strong maths and computing skills are essential because modern volcano research depends on understanding large amounts of data[/30]. Curiosity gets you started; careful analysis keeps people safe. || Presenter: Sofia, thank you very much. || Sofia: Thank you.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What originally attracted Sofia to volcanoes?

options.A: Seeing how a landscape had been created.
options.B: Watching an eruption on the news.
options.C: Finding volcanic rocks near her home.

questions.explanation: A school trip showed her a landscape formed by volcanic rock, which fascinated her.

questions.question: What does Sofia say about her normal working day?

options.A: It usually involves travelling to remote volcanoes.
options.B: It is mainly concerned with analysing information.
options.C: It is more physically dangerous than people think.

questions.explanation: She says most days are spent examining measurements on a computer.

questions.question: What does Sofia find particularly difficult?

options.A: Making uncertain risks clear to local people.
options.B: Getting communities to provide scientific data.
options.C: Predicting which equipment will survive an eruption.

questions.explanation: She highlights the challenge of communicating risk when an eruption's timing is uncertain.

questions.question: What advantage do drones provide?

options.A: They can prevent eruptions from damaging instruments.
options.B: They allow residents to see danger for themselves.
options.C: They gather information in places unsafe for researchers.

questions.explanation: Drones take samples and images from areas too dangerous to enter on foot.

questions.question: How did Sofia feel while observing an eruption?

options.A: She was mainly concerned about affected residents.
options.B: She was disappointed it was relatively small.
options.C: She felt lucky to witness such an unusual event.

questions.explanation: During the eruption, she focused on families waiting to return home safely.

questions.question: Why does Sofia value international cooperation?

options.A: It helps make public warnings identical everywhere.
options.B: It improves understanding through comparison of events.
options.C: It gives young scientists more opportunities to travel.

questions.explanation: Researchers compare eruptions from different places to recognise signs of activity sooner.

questions.question: What does Sofia recommend for future volcano scientists?

options.A: Learning how to speak several foreign languages.
options.C: Building up mathematical and computing abilities.

questions.explanation: She says modern research relies on analysing large amounts of data.
